fields.brief_block: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant must satisfy the two-prong test set forth in Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984), adopted by Georgia courts in Reynolds v. State, 306 Ga. 630 (2019).
fields.finding_analysis: The brief cites Reynolds v. State, 306 Ga. 630 (2019), but the opinion on file is Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994) -- a different case. The proposition cannot be grounded in the cited authority because the citation resolves to a different opinion. As an aside, the Black opinion does apply the Strickland two-prong framework, requiring a defendant to show that "counsel's performance was deficient" and "that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense," so the underlying legal principle is sound; but Black is not the case the brief invokes for the proposition that Georgia courts adopted Strickland, and nothing in this opinion establishes that Reynolds did so.

fields.brief_block: Georgia courts have consistently held that strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence do not amount to ineffective assistance. See Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994) [...].
fields.opinion_block: The attorney's decision to pursue the defense of insanity instead of self-defense was part of legitimate trial strategy and falls within the range of reasonable professional assistance.
fields.finding_analysis:
Black v. State supports the general principle that reasonable strategic decisions by trial counsel do not amount to ineffective assistance. The court held that counsel's choice to pursue an insanity defense over self-defense "was part of legitimate trial strategy and falls within the range of reasonable professional assistance," and quoted with approval the rule that difficult tactical decisions "with which appellant and his present counsel now disagree" do not render representation ineffective.

The fit is partial, however, because the brief frames the principle specifically around "strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence." Black does not address speculative evidence as such; its strategic-decision holding concerns the choice between two affirmative defenses (insanity vs. self-defense). The case reasonably extends to the broader proposition, but the brief's narrower "speculative evidence" formulation is not the precise holding of this opinion.

fields.brief_block: “The grant or denial of a motion for new trial is a matter within the sound discretion of the trial court and will not be disturbed if there is any evidence to authorize it.” Hargrave v. State, 311 Ga. App. 852, 855 (2011) (quoting Taylor v. State, 259 Ga. App. 457, 460 (2003)).
fields.finding_analysis:
The brief cites Taylor v. State, 259 Ga. App. 457, 460 (2003) for the standard governing the grant or denial of a motion for new trial, but the opinion on file is Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994) -- a different case. The citation resolves to a different opinion, so the cited authority cannot ground the proposition.

Separately, the quoted language does not appear anywhere in this opinion (consistent with the FABRICATED quote-check result). Black v. State addresses ineffective assistance of counsel and a motion to quash an indictment based on grand-jury disqualification; it does not state any standard of review for the grant or denial of a motion for new trial, so the subject matter is also absent from the document provided.
